signature: S-adic systems, renormalization hierarchies, and substitution dynamics for low-complexity symbolic sequences: unique-ergodicity criteria for towers of substitutions (Berthe-Delecroix / Furstenberg cone-contraction), entropy/complexity dichotomies for (random) substitution subshifts, measure-counting for linear-complexity subshifts, countable-alphabet adic towers (generalized Bratteli diagrams), and bounded-deviation (balance) theorems — hunted as the structural home of the Collatz acceleration tower, whose level-k-to-level-(k-1) substitutions are indexed by 2-adic valuations (geometric(1/2) under the Terras measure) and whose limit word is the parity itinerary.

finds.ref: Andrew Mitchell, "On word complexity and topological entropy of random substitution subshifts", 2023 (arXiv:2305.04817), Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 2024
finds.the_theorem: Thm 3.1: Let X be the subshift of a primitive random substitution (finite alphabet A; each letter maps to a FINITE set of nonempty words, one chosen with fixed probabilities; primitive: some k with every letter appearing in at least one realisation of theta^k(b) for every b). Then h_top(X)=0 iff X is the subshift of a (primitive) DETERMINISTIC substitution. Corollary 3.2 (complexity gap): zero-entropy primitive random substitution subshifts have p(n)=Theta(1) or Theta(n) — nothing between linear and exponential. This resolved the Rust-Spindeler conjecture.
finds.the_match: The Collatz acceleration IS a random substitution in the Terras measure: valuation letters v expand as v -> 1 0^{v-1} (level 1 to level 0), with v i.i.d. geometric(1/2); iterating first-returns gives the random tower. Gaps: (i) Mitchell needs finite alphabet and FINITELY many realisations per letter — our valuations are unbounded (truncation v<=V gives finite approximants, but the tail letters are exactly where divergent orbits can hide); (ii) the theorem classifies the full random subshift, not the orbit closure of one deterministic sample (a single divergent orbit); (iii) the untruncated generic object here is the full Bernoulli shift (positive entropy trivially), so the leverage is entirely on the degenerate zero-entropy corner.
finds.transfer: Target theorem: the parity word of a divergent orbit is never (aperiodic) substitutive. Chain: substitutive + zero entropy => unique letter frequency gamma = Perron eigenvector entry, hence ALGEBRAIC; Gelfond-Schneider => gamma != log2/log3 (transcendental); so log-drift gamma*log3 - log2 is strictly signed; negative => orbit bounded => cycle => eventually periodic parity, contradicting aperiodicity; positive drift is the one surviving case, to be killed with arithmetic (see best bet: Bernstein's Phi series + Mahler method). The complexity gap Theta(1)/Theta(n)-vs-exponential is the exact 'eventually periodic or positive entropy' pressure asked for in the brief, valid at the substitution-system level.

finds.ref: Valerie Berthe, Vincent Delecroix, "Beyond substitutive dynamical systems: S-adic expansions", RIMS Kokyuroku Bessatsu B46, 2014 (arXiv:1309.3960)
finds.the_theorem: Thm 5.2: a word is uniformly recurrent (orbit closure minimal) iff it admits a weakly primitive S-adic representation. Thm 5.3: finite substitution set S + strongly primitive directive sequence (exists r: sigma_n...sigma_{n+r} positive for every n) => minimal, UNIQUELY ERGODIC, at most linear complexity. Thm 5.4 (Durand): linearly recurrent iff strongly primitive AND proper S-adic. Thm 5.6 (Furstenberg-type): if a single strictly positive dxd matrix B occurs as infinitely many disjoint block-products of the incidence matrices, then the nested cones M_[0,n) R_+^d contract to one ray R_+ f = unique letter-frequency vector (Thm 3.10: the limit cone is exactly the convex hull of letter-frequency directions). Thm 4.3 (Monteil): h_X <= inf_n log(Card A_n)/beta_n^-; everywhere-growing + bounded alphabets => zero entropy. Also Ferenczi Thm 4.1: p(n+1)-p(n)<=k uniformly recurrent => S_k-adic over a FINITE substitution set.
finds.the_match: Answers transfer question (a) structurally: yes — the acceleration tower (first return to odds, then odds-of-odds, ...) is literally an S-adic expansion with computable substitutions indexed by the valuation sequence; the parity word is lim sigma_{v_0}...sigma_{v_n}(letter). Gaps, all flagged: (1) every quoted criterion assumes finite alphabets / fixed matrix dimension d — our alphabet is countable (v in N, geometric law), and the deep tail is not truncatable away for divergent orbits; (2) the directive sequence is orbit-dependent, and divergent orbits are provably ATYPICAL (limsup mean valuation <= log_2 3 < 2), so almost-sure Furstenberg arguments cannot reach them; (3) the Thm 5.6 hypothesis (recurrent positive block) is precisely a combinatorial recurrence property of the orbit's valuation word that a divergent orbit need not have; (4) BD Examples 4.4/4.5 (Cassaigne-Pytheas Fogg) warn that computable/primitive substitutions alone control nothing — growth and properness conditions are essential.
finds.transfer: Concrete provable statement: if a divergent orbit has BOUNDED valuations and its valuation word is linearly recurrent (equivalently by Thm 5.4: strongly primitive proper S-adic), then its orbit closure is uniquely ergodic with an exact parity frequency f_1 given by cone contraction (Thm 5.6), the drift is exact, and the LR rigidity + Gelfond-Schneider exclusion of the critical density log2/log3 (in the eventually-periodic-directive = substitutive case, where f is algebraic) forces the strict-drift dichotomy of Find 1. Deliverable: 'no divergent orbit has a linearly recurrent parity word' — one full rung above the known 'no eventually periodic parity word' (= no cycles escape).

finds.ref: Van Cyr, Bryna Kra, "Counting generic measures for a subshift of linear growth", J. Eur. Math. Soc. 21 (2019), 355-380 (arXiv:1505.02748)
finds.the_theorem: Thm 1.1: if (X,sigma) is ANY subshift (no minimality!) with liminf_n P_X(n)/n < k for some integer k, then X has at most k-1 distinct nonatomic GENERIC measures (measures with a generic point; strictly more than ergodic measures). Thm 1.2: if limsup P_X(n)/n < k and some generic point's orbit closure is not uniquely ergodic, at most k-2. Background (Boshernitzan 1984): minimal + limsup P(n)/n < 3 => uniquely ergodic. Bounds are sharp (Thm 1.3).
finds.the_match: X = orbit closure of the parity word e of a divergent orbit. e is aperiodic (eventually periodic parity => convergence to a rational 2-adic cycle; for an integer orbit that means entering a cycle, excluded by divergence), so Morse-Hedlund gives P_e(n) >= n+1: the complexity floor. Divergence forces every subsequential Birkhoff limit mu of e to satisfy mu([1]) >= log2/log3 ~ 0.6309 (running odd-density >= log2/log3 - O(1/n) since the value stays >= 1). The theorem applies verbatim — no minimality needed, which is crucial since single-orbit closures are rarely minimal. Gap, flagged hard: Cyr-Kra bounds the NUMBER of generic measures, not their values; nothing symbolic pins any mu([1]) at 1/2, so no contradiction without an arithmetic input. Also e itself need not be a generic point (its averages may not converge) — the theorem still counts its subsequential limits.
finds.transfer: Pincer strategy: prove any divergent parity word has liminf P(n)/n = infinity by excluding each low-complexity stratum with arithmetic. Immediate rung: liminf P_e(n)/n < 3 => at most 2 nonatomic generic measures, all with odd-density in [log2/log3, 1] — a bizarre, highly structured object (near-minimal, almost balanced, high 1-density) that should collide with the transduction constraint (invariant set avoiding 1); our 7-state-DFA no-go is the automaton shadow of exactly this stratum.

finds.ref: Sebastian Donoso, Fabien Durand, Alejandro Maass, Samuel Petite, "Interplay between finite topological rank minimal Cantor systems, S-adic subshifts and their complexity", Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. 374 (2021), 3453-3489 (arXiv:2003.06328)
finds.the_theorem: Expansive minimal Cantor systems of finite topological rank coincide, up to conjugacy, with primitive recognizable S-adic subshifts of bounded alphabet rank; the paper gives necessary and sufficient conditions for a minimal subshift to have finite topological rank. Application: every minimal subshift with non-superlinear complexity (liminf p(n)/n < infinity) has finite topological rank — i.e., ALL classical zero-entropy examples are finitely-parametrized S-adic objects.
finds.the_match: This is the structure theorem that turns 'low complexity' into 'finitely describable certificate'. If a divergent parity word had liminf p(n)/n < infinity AND minimal orbit closure, that closure would be a finite-rank primitive recognizable S-adic subshift — exactly one rung above regular languages, the next certificate class after our theorem that no regular invariant set of the transduction T avoiding 1 exists with <=7 DFA states. Gaps: (i) minimality of a single-orbit closure is a real hypothesis, not free (non-minimal low-complexity closures exist; Cyr-Kra covers those but without the structure theorem); (ii) passing from 'parity word of one orbit' to 'invariant set / certificate' needs our transduction bookkeeping, which is outside their framework; (iii) recognizability is a hypothesis in the converse direction only — the forward direction (complexity => finite rank) is clean.
finds.transfer: Extend the certificate no-go up the ladder: 'no divergence certificate is a finite-topological-rank minimal subshift', equivalently 'any divergent parity word with minimal orbit closure has superlinear complexity'. First move: run DDMP's finite-rank conditions against the constraint that the subshift is carried by the rational transduction T avoiding 1 — the finite data (bounded alphabet rank, recognizable morphism tower) should be exhaustively searchable the same way we searched <=7-state DFAs; a finite-rank certificate search with a no-go proof would be a genuine strengthening.

finds.ref: Sergey Bezuglyi, Olena Karpel, Jan Kwiatkowski, Marcin Wata, "Inverse limit method for generalized Bratteli diagrams and invariant measures", 2024 (arXiv:2404.14654); with companions Bezuglyi-Karpel 'Subdiagrams and invariant measures on Bratteli diagrams' (arXiv:1502.05690, 1311.3266) and the generalized Bratteli-Vershik realization of aperiodic Borel automorphisms
finds.the_theorem: For GENERALIZED Bratteli diagrams — countably infinite vertex set at every level — the set of tail-invariant probability measures is identified with the inverse limit of the infinite-dimensional simplices attached to levels (maps given by transposed incidence matrices); all ergodic tail-invariant probability measures are explicitly described for classes including the infinite Pascal graph, diagrams built from countable families of odometers, and reducible diagrams with uncountably many ergodic measures. Companion theorems (Bezuglyi-Karpel): exact criteria for when a SUBDIAGRAM supports a finite tail-invariant measure of positive mass ('thin' subdiagrams support none), via products of incidence-matrix norms.
finds.the_match: This is the missing countable-alphabet version of Berthe-Delecroix Thm 5.6 (finite-d Perron cone contraction), and the exact formal home of our tower: the Collatz acceleration with valuation alphabet N at every level is a STATIONARY generalized Bratteli diagram; the Terras measure is the tail-invariant measure with geometric(1/2) edge weights; a divergence certificate (invariant set of T avoiding 1) is precisely a SUBDIAGRAM, and 'does it carry mass / an invariant measure with odd-density >= log2/log3' is precisely their subdiagram-measure criterion. Gaps: their explicit classifications so far cover Pascal/odometer-type diagrams, not ours; tail-invariance vs shift-invariance needs careful bookkeeping (tail-invariant on the path space vs T-invariant measures); and the divergent set has Terras measure zero, so measure classification constrains certificates, not individual orbits.
finds.transfer: Two computations to run: (1) apply the inverse-limit method to the stationary Collatz valuation diagram and classify its ergodic tail-invariant measures — conjecture: a one-parameter Bernoulli/Markov family plus cycle-supported atoms, with the drift functional separating them; (2) feed a hypothetical certificate subdiagram (invariant, avoiding 1, drift >= log2/log3) into the Bezuglyi-Karpel thin-subdiagram criterion to prove no certificate subdiagram supports finite invariant mass — a measure-theoretic no-go complementing our regular-language no-go.

finds.ref: Boris Adamczewski, "Balances for fixed points of primitive substitutions", Theoret. Comput. Sci. 307 (2003), 47-75 (companion: Bufetov-Solomyak limit theorems / deviation of Birkhoff sums for substitution systems)
finds.the_theorem: Let u be a fixed point of a primitive substitution with incidence matrix M, dominant eigenvalue theta_1. If every other eigenvalue has modulus < 1 (Pisot-type condition), then u is C-balanced: discrepancy sup_N |(number of a's in u_1..u_N) - N*freq(a)| <= C, uniformly. In general the deviation grows like N^{log|theta_2|/log theta_1} (up to log factors) when |theta_2|>1 — a complete trichotomy: bounded / polynomial exponent from the spectrum / (for non-primitive or frequency-gap cases) linear.
finds.the_match: Antihydra instantiation: the counter is the Birkhoff sum of the weight w(even)=+2, w(odd)=-1 along the parity word of the floor(3n/2)-orbit, and halting = the sum hitting an exact boundary against drift +1/2. If the parity word were substitutive of Pisot type, the counter would equal N*drift +/- C forever, so it could cross the boundary only inside a finite, explicitly computable window — NON-HALTING WOULD BECOME A FINITE CHECK. Gaps, flagged hard: nobody expects Antihydra's parity word to be substitutive (Dubickas's not-Sturmian theorem points the other way); the theorem gives zero leverage on unstructured words; even the existence of the frequency (drift) for the actual orbit is open — this is a conditional-structure result, i.e., it maps out what the enemy CANNOT look like.
finds.transfer: Deviation-based obstruction hierarchy: prove 'if Antihydra halts at step N, then its parity prefix has discrepancy >= c*N (explicit c from the drift 1/2 and the surplus at the boundary)', so halting forces LINEAR discrepancy — placing any halting trajectory in the maximal-deviation class of the Adamczewski/Bufetov-Solomyak trichotomy, excluding en masse all Pisot-substitutive, LR, and bounded-discrepancy S-adic structures for the prefix. Same template for Space Needle: value+v2-valuation hitting an exact power of 2 is a boundary-hit of a Birkhoff sum with drift, so every bounded-deviation structural class is excluded by one inequality.

best_single_bet: Play out Find 1 + Find 2 (Mitchell dichotomy + BD S-adic rigidity) into the theorem "no divergent Collatz orbit has an aperiodic substitutive parity word" — the exact next rung above the known no-eventually-periodic rung, and the substitutive shadow of our no-regular-certificate result. First move (day 1, one page): prove the drift-exclusion lemma — a substitutive parity word has algebraic letter frequency gamma (Perron eigenvector entry, Adamczewski gives the discrepancy control), Gelfond-Schneider makes log2/log3 transcendental, so gamma != log2/log3 and the drift gamma*log3 - log2 is strictly signed; negative drift bounds the orbit => cycle => eventually periodic => contradicts aperiodicity. That leaves exactly one case: strictly positive algebraic drift. Days 2-5: kill it arithmetically — write Bernstein's non-iterative series Phi(e) in Z_2 (bernstein-1994-noniterative-2adic.pdf is already in /Users/simon/Desktop/COLLATZ/papers/) for a 2-automatic parity vector e, derive the Mahler-type functional equation its generating series satisfies, and use the p-adic Mahler-method machinery already collected in the folder (adamczewski-bell-mahler-problem-1303.2019.pdf, schafke-singer-2016) to show Phi(e) cannot be a positive integer for aperiodic automatic e — first numerically (compute Phi(Thue-Morse), Phi(Fibonacci-word) to 200 2-adic digits and test rationality via digit-periodicity), then structurally.
dead_ends: (1) The naive single-orbit dichotomy "itineraries of expanding(-on-average) affine maps are eventually periodic or have positive entropy" is FALSE as stated and not in the literature for good reason: Sturmian and other zero-entropy aperiodic itineraries embed in full shifts and beta-shifts (kneading theory realizes them), so no purely symbolic/dynamical theorem of this shape exists — the dichotomy only holds at the substitution-SYSTEM level (Mitchell, primitive random substitutions) or under minimality+finite-rank hypotheses (DDMP); any Collatz version MUST consume arithmetic input (integrality). Do not re-search for it. (2) Almost-sure unique ergodicity for random S-adic systems (Furstenberg random matrix products over the Terras/geometric directive law) is structurally useless for divergence: divergent orbits form a measure-zero, provably atypical set (limsup mean valuation <= log_2 3 < 2), so only deterministic recurrence hypotheses (BD Thm 5.6 positive-block recurrence) can ever reach them. (3) BD Examples 4.4/4.5 (Cassaigne-Pytheas Fogg): computability/primitivity of the individual substitutions in the tower controls NOTHING about the limit word's complexity without growth+properness conditions — transfer (a) cannot be won at the level of "the tower is S-adic with computable substitutions" alone; that statement is true but empty. (4) Akiyama-Frougny-Sakarovitch rational-base numeration (checked: Collatz-blind, non-regular non-context-free language L_{3/2}, Mahler connection) and the matching/alpha-continued-fraction strand (Bruin-Carminati-Kalle) are strong cousins but were already collected by sibling agents (files predating this session in /Users/simon/Desktop/COLLATZ/papers/) — no need to re-search either strand. (5) Generalized Bratteli measure classification cannot see individual divergent orbits (measure zero); its leverage is exclusively against certificate SUBDIAGRAMS — don't try to use it orbit-by-orbit.
